title: Regulatory Intensity and Firm-Specific Exposure
abstract: Building on administrative data and machine-learning models, I develop a firm-specific measure of regulatory intensity: cost of compliance with all federal paperwork regulations. Regulatory intensity increases the cost of goods sold and overhead spending (SGA). It also incentivizes companies to reduce capital investment, hire fewer employees, and lobby more. The effects are particularly strong among financially constrained firms and those with irreversible investment opportunities, suggesting that regulation affects companies through budgetary pressures and heightened uncertainty. The findings highlight the real effects of regulation and the underlying mechanisms.

author.Joseph Kalmenovitz*: Simon Business School, University of Rochester, USA

article.1. Background and Data Sources:
JEL E22 - Investment; Capital; Intangible Capital; Capacity G18 - Government Policy and Regulation G28 - Government Policy and Regulation G31 - Capital Budgeting; Fixed Investment and Inventory Studies; Capacity K23 - Regulated Industries and Administrative Law Issue Section: Article Editor: Ralph Koijen Ralph Koijen Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Regulation is a fundamental economic concept. Scholars, politicians, and media commentators often discuss regulation broadly or in the context of specific rules. High-level normative debates and rule-level analyses are important, but missing is a rigorous measure that consistently quantifies the intensity of a wide range of regulations. Consequently, it is difficult to evaluate different theories of regulation and explore empirically how regulation affects economic decisions. In this paper, I take one step in that direction. I measure the cost of compliance with all federal paperwork regulations, using machine-learning techniques and a novel administrative data set, and then study the impact of regulatory burden on key economic outcomes.1

As of December 2020, federal agencies collectively impose 9,553 forms on the public: corporations must file tax returns, truck drivers record on-duty hours, patients and doctors fill out privacy forms, manufacturers report working conditions, and so on. The abundance of paperwork is reputed to impose a heavy economic toll on companies. One example is a 2019Wall Street JournalEditorial, warning of a “real threat to more than 5 million small businesses” because of an “overly broad and dubious” paperwork burden on business owners (WSJ 2019). Less known is the fact that federal agencies are legally required to monitor the cost of compliance with each paperwork regulation. For example, as of April 2021, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission reported that companies spend 14.2 million hours every year to comply with the disclosure requirements in Form 10-K (annual financial statement). That includes the time taken to search for data, compile the materials, and review and file the form with the SEC. Since paperwork regulations are abundant and costly, and are also measured and monitored uniformly, they offer a unique opportunity to study the economics of regulation.

In the first part of the paper, I analyze aggregate regulatory burden. I hand-collect information from the original reports prepared by federal agencies between January 1980 and December 2020, and construct a panel that tracks the costs of compliance across each regulation. I convert the panel into three measures, each of which reflects the aggregate regulatory intensity at any point in time: the number of active regulations, the estimated number of responses (“how much paperwork”), and the hours spent on compliance. Several stylized facts highlight the economic burden of regulation and its relation to other macroeconomic factors. Over the entire sample period, the public spent292.1billion hours on preparing and filing2.24trillion forms, to comply with36,702regulations. In an average year, compliance consumes3.2%of total working hours in the United States. Regulatory intensity has been declining since August 2016, and as of December 2020 has reached its prefinancial crisis levels of September 2008. Regulatory intensity increases during Democratic administrations, and is negatively correlated with gross domestic product (GDP) growth and other economic indicators. Put differently, regulatory intensity is countercyclical: it increases (decreases) when economic conditions worsen (improve).2

In the second part of the paper, I develop the primary measures of firm-specific regulatory intensity. Each company is subject to a different subset of regulations, and I capture this heterogeneity using supervised machine-learning algorithms. The methodology consists of three steps. First, I build a training sample for each company: a small set of regulations, split evenly between regulations that are relevant and irrelevant for that company. To construct the training samples, I compute the cosine similarity between the company’s 10-K report and each rule,3and select the rules with the highest and lowest cosine scores. For example, Boeing’s training sample includes safety regulations of the Federal Aviation Administration (relevant), and derivatives trading rules of the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (irrelevant). Next, I use the training sample to estimate a supervised machine-learning algorithm. The machine-learning algorithm identifies linguistic patterns of relevant and irrelevant regulations in the training sample, and then classifies the remaining regulations as relevant or irrelevant for that company.4Finally, I calculate the company’s regulatory burden solely based on the regulations that are deemed relevant to that company.

The three firm-specific measures, like the aggregate ones, represent the quantity of active regulations and the estimated costs of compliance in terms of responses and hours. The three measures are highly correlated and deliver similar results throughout the paper. However, the two based on responses and hours reflect how regulators perceive the burden. It is difficult to independently verify those estimates, and in fact, the paucity of independent measures has motivated this paper. To alleviate the concern, I examine the internal procedures in four agencies that produce a large portion of the burden. In general, while measurement error seems unavoidable, numerous checks and balances should limit its scope. For example, the burden estimates conform to government-wide uniform guidelines, and are subject to public inspection and to a two-stage peer-review process, by an independent team inside the agency and by a designated White House bureau.

In the third part of the paper, I study the economic impact of regulatory intensity. Two overarching theories of regulation guide the empirical analysis. On one hand,public interesttheories postulate that regulation is a welfare-increasing institution designed to correct market failures, such as monopoly power and asymmetric information (Pigou 1938;Demsetz 1974;Joskow and Rose 1989;Melody 2016). The alternative theories, often known aspublic choice,private interestorregulatory capture, view regulation as a rent-seeking process whereby private actors advance their self-interests at the expense of the public good (Tullock 1967;Stigler 1971;Krueger 1974;Posner 1974;Peltzman 1976;Becker 1983;Benmelech and Moskowitz 2010).5

While the two theories differ with respect to the benefits of regulation, both seem to agree that regulation is costly for firms. Thus, they provide a useful road map to validate the new measure. Specifically, I present three sets of results.First, companies with heavy regulatory burden spend significantly larger fraction of their revenues on SGA (sales, general, and administrative) and COGS (cost of goods sold).Second, I investigate plausible responses to rising compliance costs. I find that regulatory intensity leads to more lobbying and less hiring. To finance rising compliance costs, companies reduce cash holdings, issue new debt, and substantially increase their leverage.Third, cross-sectional tests highlight two possible mechanisms: budget constraints and uncertainty. Compliance costs could create budget pressures, forcing companies to prioritize compliance over other business activities (Giroud and Mueller 2017). Moreover, the expansion of regulatory burden increases the legal uncertainty, incentivizing managers to postpone projects until the uncertainty would be resolved (McDonald and Siegel 1986;Bernanke 1983;Julio and Yook 2012;Gulen and Ion 2015). Indeed, I find that the decline in capital investment is concentrated among financially constrained firms that have little slack and must repurpose resources toward compliance, and among companies with irreversible investment opportunities that are especially sensitive to uncertainty.

All the empirical findings are identified within-firm over time, net of industry trends (firm and year×industry fixed effects), and conditional on standard controls, such as size and cash flow. The results are similar using the three measures ofRegIn⁠, and they are robust to various permutations of the baseline methodology. For instance, I use additional excerpts from 10-K filings to compute the cosine scores, and consider nine different machine-learning algorithms to classify the remaining regulations. The results do not change when I control for two proxies of regulatory burden: one based on the number of words in the Code of Federal Regulation that apply to the company’s industry (Al-Ubaydli and McLaughlin 2017), and another based on the number of words in the company’s annual report associated with regulation (Gong and Yannelis 2018). Those additional controls are available for only19%of the sample, and nevertheless all the results remain economically large and statistically significant.

In sum, regulatory intensity leads to material adjustments of corporate policies. The results are broadly consistent with theories of regulation, suggesting that regulatory burden increases overhead costs and the net present value of political investment (Bertrand, Bombardini, and Trebbi 2014), at the expense of other projects. They are also consistent with anecdotal evidence on the burden of paperwork compliance.6These findings help validate the new measure, but should not be interpreted as conclusive verdict against the value of regulation. First, causal inference is challenging because regulation is rarely imposed on companies exogenously. It resembles a cat-and-mouse game, where companies adjust their business models in response to regulatory actions, regulators initiate actions to address new developments among regulated companies, and both sides continuously interact with each other through lobbying, comment letters, and meetings. Second, many paperwork regulations will likely have significant positive externalities. Aviation safety rules protect travelers and air crews, and coal mines inspections protect workers, to mention just two examples. Full investigation of those benefits is beyond the scope of this paper, but they must be ultimately weighed against any potential costs. Thus, while theories of regulation are helpful in guiding the analysis, one should be cautious when drawing final conclusions from the empirical findings presented here.

There is a growing recognition of the need to better understand how regulation broadly affects economic activity. Against the backdrop of this important challenge, I make two contributions. First, I add a set of measures, which seek to quantify regulatory burden. Existing studies typically utilize two types of data. “Supply” measures focus on textual output of regulators, such as the number of rules and words (La Porta, Lopez-de Silanes, Shleifer, and Vishny 1998;Botero, Djankov, Porta, Lopez-de Silanes, and Shleifer 2004;Mulligan and Shleifer 2005;Al-Ubaydli and McLaughlin 2017) or their textual complexity (Amadxarif, Brookes, Garbarino, Patel, and Walczak 2019;Colliard and Georg 2020;De Lucio and Mora-Sanguinetti 2021). “Demand” measures focus on data disclosed by companies, for example, industry-level spending on compliance occupations (Simkovic and Zhang 2019) and keywords usage, such as “regulation” (Calomiris, Mamaysky, and Yang 2020;Gong and Yannelis 2018). My approach combines both sources: I measure the burden of each regulation using detailed administrative data (“supply”), and match it to regulated entities based on how they describe themselves (“demand”). This approach has several advantages. First, it does not require companies to explicitly discuss regulation in their corporate filings or to use specific keywords from a predetermined list.7Instead, it is suffice to compare the vocabularies used by regulators and the company in its corporate filings. Second, my approach differentiates between regulation and deregulation (increase vs. decrease), and more importantly between costly regulations and less-costly ones. This is important, because even if the firm mentions both Regulation X and Regulation Y, it does not mean that both regulations have the same impact on its operations. By linking each regulation to its official burden estimates, I am able to overcome this challenge. Third, the underlying data set (83-I forms) is publicly available and updated continuously. Fourth, the data set is flexible and can be adjusted for specific lines of research: the burdens can be calculated at various frequencies and for subsets of regulations and agencies. Lastly, linking agencies to regulations to companies opens the door for further studies on the political economy of regulation. For example, how the incentives of individual regulators spill over to companies via changes in regulation.

Second, I contribute to the literature on the economic impact of regulation. This topic is the subject of many policy discussions and broad theories (cited above), ranging frompublic interesttoregulatory capture. I pursue a middle road, in between high-level discussions and rule-specific analyses, and estimate the impact of a large body of federal regulations on key company-level outcomes. Facing surging costs due to higher regulatory intensity, companies concentrate on a smaller number of less-regulated business segments, accelerate lobbying spending, and pull resources away from capital investment and hiring. Further analysis highlights the role of budgetary constraints and legal uncertainty, which could amplify the economic impact of regulation. Combined, these results shed more light on the real effects of regulation and the underlying mechanisms.
1.1 Paperwork regulations

Federal regulators collect plenty of information from the public. Manufacturers provide information on the materials they use, public companies disclose their financial status and business operations, commercial transportation companies record the hours drivers spend operating their vehicles, and so on. The information is collected on physical or electronic forms, a process that creates an enormous burden of federal paperwork.

Federal paperwork regulations are generated and monitored in a centralized process prescribed by the Paperwork Reduction Act, or PRA (44 U.S.C. 3501), and by the Code of Federal Regulations (5 CFR 1320). The terms “paperwork” and “information collection” are used interchangeably to describe the kind of regulations that are subject to the PRA. The scope of the PRA is very broad and it encompasses a wide range of economic activities. The paperwork regulations covered by the PRA include transmission of information to the government (e.g., tax forms and grant applications), record-keeping requirements (e.g., small businesses keeping all tax-related documents for 3 years), and third-party disclosures (e.g., annual financial reports by public companies).

The process begins when the federal agency prepares an information collection request. An independent team within the agency reviews the request and publishes a 60-day notice in the Federal Register seeking comments from the public. Upon completion of the internal review, the agency publishes another 30-day notice in the Federal Register and concurrently submits the request to the Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs (OIRA), a designated unit within the White House’s Office of Management and Budget (OMB). Desk officers at OIRA review the request and decide whether to approve the collection with or without changes. If approved, the collection is assigned a unique OMB control number and an renewal date (left panel ofFigure 1). To renew or revise an existing collection, the agency must repeat the whole process.8

The backbone of the entire review process is Form 83-I (Figure 1). This form, and the accompanying “Supporting Statement,” are at the center of the request prepared by the agency and reviewed externally by OIRA. It describes the purpose and scope of the collection and the affected entities. Among other items, the agency provides an estimate of the burden the collection imposes on the public. The three burden metrics areResponses⁠,Time⁠, andDollar⁠. The agency must report how many responses it will receive per year, how many hours it will take the public to comply with the regulation, and what would be the dollar costs of compliance. Consider, for example, Form 10-K, the annual report public companies prepare. According to a recent Form 83-I (as of April 2021), the annual number of 10-K filings is 8,286 and companies spend in total 14.2 million hours and $1.895 billion annually to comply with 10-K requirements. Per OMB guidelines, it includes the costs of searching for data, compiling the materials, and filing the 10-K form with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission. It also includes the costs of acquiring technology and training personnel. Similarly, the burden associated with tax returns includes not only the costs of filling the forms but also those associated with hiring a professional advisor, collecting the information, and purchasing the necessary software.

1.2 Data

I scraped from OIRA’s website all 83-I forms filed between 1971 and 2020. That yields a raw data set of181,539forms filed in connection with 37,279 separate regulations.9I collect from each Form 83-I the contemporary burden estimates per each regulation, as well as process-related information, such as purpose (e.g., initiating a new collection); review type (regular or expedited); conclusion (e.g., approval without change); and timeline (request date, conclusion date, and new expiration date).

Finally, I convert the form-level data into a panel of regulations (regulation×year observations). The main set of variables is the annualized burden, based on the number of responses, hours of compliance, and dollar costs of compliance. All burden measures are based on the estimates from the latest available 83-I. If a new 83-I was filed mid-year, I use the weighted average between the previous 83-I and the new one. For example, suppose the annualized hour burden as of January 1, 2009, was100⁠, and in March 1, 2009, a new 83-I request put the annualized hour burden on120⁠. The annualized burden for 2009 would be116.7(⁠16⋅100+56⋅120⁠). Throughout the paper, all dollar values are adjusted for inflation (in $US(2017)).

Table 1summarizes the regulation-level burden. Between 1981 and 2020, federal agencies imposed36,702regulations. The average regulation was active for 10 years, and over its entire lifetime triggered 2.6 million responses, 1.1 million working hours, and $298 million in expenses. Every year,7.3%of the regulations are retired, but for each eliminated regulation1.3new regulations are introduced, creating an upward time trend in regulation. TheInternet Appendix (Table A.1)lists some of the most burdensome regulations. For instance, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services is in charge of annual reports filed by hospitals, nursing homes, and home health agencies. Those reports must include comprehensive financial statements as well as a slew of information on health care standards in the facility.

It is difficult to independently verify the agencies’ burden estimates. In fact, the scarcity of such measures motivated this paper. To better understand the validity of the burden estimates, I study the internal review processes in some of the most prolific agencies (in terms of regulatory burden). I discuss those processes at length in Section A.1. Generally, the extensive checks and balances installed throughout the government should mitigate the concerns of potential measurement error. Burden estimation has been practiced by federal agencies for many decades. The estimates are compiled by the agency’s specialists based on government-wide uniform guidelines. The estimates are inspected internally by an independent team, and reviewed externally by desk officers at OIRA. The public is invited to inspect and comment on the burden estimates, during the formal notices procedure and meetings with agency officials. OIRA submits to Congress an annual Information Collection Budget, which provides an updated picture of the regulatory burden and the extent to which agencies have reduced the burden. Finally, the entire process, like many other aspects of government operations, is being audited periodically by the Government Accountability Office.
article.2. Aggregate Regulatory Intensity:
The data set described above, which is based on Form 83-I, provides regulation-level information. The next step is to aggregate the individual burdens into a comprehensive index of regulatory intensity. In this section I discuss the aggregate measures and present new stylized facts. In the next section, I will turn to more granular measures of intensity at the firm and industry level.

2.1 Measuring regulatory intensity

To measure aggregate regulatory intensity, I follow the methodology ofBaker, Bloom, and Davis (2016). I provide here a brief description and defer the full technical discussion to theInternet Appendix (Section A.2).

To calculate the regulatory intensity at timet⁠, I start with the panel of regulations described in Section 1.2. I convert it into a panel of agencies, where each observation represents the burden imposed by agencyaat timet⁠. I standardize and winsorize the values, to make burden estimates comparable and to reduce the impact of extreme outliers. I then sum the values across all agencies within timet⁠, creating a government time-series. Finally, I normalize the government series to a mean of 100, producing the finalRegIntindex. It represents the burden of compliance, during timet⁠, with federal paperwork regulations. Three versions ofRegIntcorrespond to three notions of burden from Form 83-I: active regulations, number of responses, and hours of compliance. The dollar-based estimates were added after 1996 and are available for less than 20%of the regulations. Therefore, to conduct a more cautious analysis, I focus only on the three “baseline” aspects of burden.

The three measures of regulatory intensity are highly correlated in the time-series (⁠0.87−0.97⁠). Conceptually, they offer different advantages. Counting regulations is a transparent and easily replicable measure of regulation. It assumes that the units of regulation, essentially the number of laws, is a meaningful metric. Numerous studies seem to support this simplifying assumption, primarily by documenting a significant correlation between counting-based regulation measures and various economic outcomes.10If those measures contained no information, they should not be systematically related to dependent variables of interest. However, not all regulations are born equal, and some are more complex and burdensome than others. This motivates the alternative measures I present that rely on official estimated costs. The idea was pioneered byDjankov, La Porta, Lopez-de Silanes, and Shleifer (2002), who measure cross-country regulation of entry in 1997 based on the official estimates of the time taken and costs required to fulfill licensing procedures.Simkovic and Zhang (2019)use a somewhat similar strategy. They estimate industry-level labor expenses on regulation-related tasks. Compared to the “plain” measure of active regulations, the latter two measures explicitly address the heterogeneity across regulations by calculating the estimated compliance costs. The key challenge, though, is in the word “estimated.” The data in the official 83-I reports reflect how the regulators perceive the burden, and perceptions might differ from the true burden borne by the compliers (see discussion in Section 1.2).

2.2 Stylized facts

In this section I provide new stylized facts on the aggregate intensity of federal regulations. TheInternet Appendixcontains additional information (Section A.3).

On aggregate,36,702paperwork regulations triggered2.24trillion responses and292.1billion hours.Figure 2adds some perspective. The figure plots the annual compliance burden as a share of total hours worked by all U.S. part-time and full-time employees (from the St. Louis Fed’s website). In an average year, compliance with paperwork regulations alone accounts for3.2%of total working hours. The share of compliance costs has surged during 1989-1990, and again during 2005-2006.11

The tasks of drafting, interpreting, and enforcing federal regulations are carried out by206individual federal agencies. Indeed, a growing literature shows that organizational features of regulatory agencies affect their performance.12Because the intensity measures are based on a rich administrative data set, future research can focus on subsets of federal agencies and regulations. To demonstrate this property, panel B ofFigure 3plots the intensity imposed by 12 financial regulators.13The “financial” index is highly correlated with the general one (⁠0.83−0.93⁠), and shows a similar deregulation trend since 2016 and a similar uptick during the COVID-19 crisis. Two differences in the time-series are worth pointing out: a wave of financial deregulation from 1982 to 1985 which preceded the general deregulation of 1986-1988, and a spike in financial regulation during 2009 and again during 2011 following the financial crisis and Dodd-Frank Act respectively.

Table 2reports univariate correlation between regulatory intensity and macroeconomic factors. Regulation is positively correlated with the size of the federal workforce (data fromChen and Kalmenovitz (2021)), as expected, given that federal employees are drafting and implementing those regulations. Regulatory burden is negatively correlated with Republican administrations, consistent with the perception that GOP presidents seek to reduce regulatory burden. Regulation is countercyclical, that is, it increases (declines) when macroeconomic conditions worsen (improve). This relationship is present with respect to GDP growth, returns on the S&P 500 index, and economic optimism from the University of Michigan’s consumer survey. These time-series correlations do not imply that regulation necessarily causes or contributes to the economic decline. I will examine these possibilities in greater detail in Section 4. Regulatory intensity is positively correlated with the number of words in the Code of Federal Regulations (CFR), the annual publication of all federal regulations (data fromAl-Ubaydli and McLaughlin (2017)). This is not surprising, since paperwork regulations are a subset of all federal regulations.14Surprisingly, though, I find a negative correlation between regulatory intensity and CFR restrictions, which is the percentage of restrictive words in the CFR (such as “shall” or “must”). This illustrates a potential limitation of text-based measures: even as the number of “textual” restrictions is declining, the actual burden of compliance is increasing across the board. Finally, there is a significant positive relation between regulations and the Economic Policy Uncertainty index (EPU) developed byBaker, Bloom, and Davis (2016). The EPU uses media reports to proxy for policy-related economic uncertainty. One possible interpretation is that a flow of new regulations generates uncertainty among firms, since it is unknown how those regulations would be interpreted and enforced (Battalio and Schultz 2011;Baker, Bloom, Canes-Wrone, Davis, and Rodden 2014). I will return to this fact in Section 4.4.

article.3. Firm-Level Regulatory Intensity:
Paperwork regulations do not apply uniformly to all companies. For example, a manufacturing company is likely more sensitive to EPA regulations, while a bank holding company is more exposed to regulations enforced by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporations. To capture this heterogeneity, I use supervised machine-learning algorithms to predict which regulation is plausibly relevant to each firm’s operations.

3.1 Classifying regulations

The first task is to determine whether paperwork regulationris relevant to companyi⁠, for each pair ofrandi⁠. For brevity, I outline below only the main steps, and defer the formal methodological discussion to theInternet Appendix (Section A.4).

First, I describe each company based on its corporate disclosure forms. I obtain 10-K reports from Bill McDonald’s website. From those reports I extract Item 1, where the company must describe its products, services, and legal environment. Per the SEC’s recommendation, Item 1 “is a good place to start to understand how the company operates.”15I aggregate the information across all years, creating word collections that capture how each company describes its own operations. Similarly, I describe each regulation using the original text of Form 83-I with the regulation’s name, abstract, and responsive agencies. To match the availability of the 10-K files, I focus on the25,950regulations that were in effect at any point between 1994 and 2020.

Next, I calculate the cosine similarity between each pair of company×regulation. The intuition is to see whether companyiand regulationruse a similar vocabulary. Based on the cosine scores, I construct a training sample for each firm: a set of200regulations, split evenly between regulations that are relevant and irrelevant for that firm. Specifically, I obtain residual cosine scores by subtracting the rule’s average score from the raw score (this accounts for the fact that some regulations use boilerplate language that resembles many companies). I then select the100regulations with the highest residual scores (relevant), and randomly pick100regulations with negative residual scores (irrelevant). For instance, Boeing is a large aerospace manufacturer. In Boeing’s training sample, relevant regulations (high residual cosine) include various safety and inspection regulations by the Federal Aviation Administration; pollution regulations by the Environmental Protection Agency; and disclosure of loan or sale of aircraft equipment parts by the Bureau of Industry and Security. Irrelevant regulations (negative residual cosine) include derivatives trading rules by the Commodity Futures Trading Commission; foot-and-mouth disease disclosure requirements by the Department of Agriculture; student loan regulations from the Department of Education; and license forms issued by the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, and Firearms. The machine-learning algorithm studies the training sample, identifies linguistic patterns of relevant versus irrelevant regulations, and then scans each of the remaining regulations to determine if it resembles relevant regulations more than it resembles irrelevant ones.

I consider nine machine-learning classifiers (as inChen, Wu, and Yang 2019). The first eight are naïve Bayes, k-nearest neighbors, random forest, decision tree, gradient boost, linear support vector classification (SVC), Gaussian SVC, and logistic regression. The ninth is a “voting” classifier, which aggregates predictions of the decision tree, gradient boost, and linear SVC. I fit each model to the training sample and evaluate its out-of-sample performance based on standard metrics.Table 3reports the results. The voting classifier achieves the highest scores across all metrics, with97.3%precision and96.8%accuracy.16Therefore, this is the model I use throughout the paper (all the models largely agree on the classification; the average correlation across models is0.85⁠). I fit the voting model to the training sample and then classify the remainder of the25,950paperwork regulations.

3.2 Computing regulatory intensity

Having classified the paperwork regulations, I turn to construct a granular version ofRegInt⁠. The methodology is almost identical to the one described in Section 2.1, but instead of creatingRegIntbased on all existing regulations, I computeRegIni,tsolely based on regulations that are relevant for firmi⁠. As before, the resultant set ofRegIni,tmeasures represent the quantity of active regulations and their estimated compliance costs: total responses (“how much paperwork”) and total hours spent on compliance. The baseline measures are annual, to match the availability of many corporate outcomes on Compustat. Analogous daily, monthly, and quarterly indexes can be used by scholars to explore additional research directions.17

To supplement the analysis, I construct two alternative proxies for regulatory burden. The first is based on Al-Ubaydli and McLaughlin (2017) , who classify the Code of Federal Regulations by two digit NAICS industries. For each CFR part, 18 the authors report the probability that it applies to industry j and the number of words in that part. By multiplying the probability with the number of words and aggregating across all CFR parts, I obtain the CFR burden on industry j at time t ( ⁠ C F R j , t ⁠ ). I then define the firm-level CFR burden, denoted C F R i , t ⁠ , by taking into account the company’s activities across different business segments. Formally, C F R i , t = ∑ j ∈ J t ω i , j , t ⋅ C F R j , t , (1) where J t is the set of two digit NAICS industries in which firm i operates during time t ⁠ , and ω i , j , t is the share of firm i ’s revenues derived from industry j at time t (based on Compustat’s business segment data). 19 The second proxy follows Gong and Yannelis (2018) and Calomiris, Mamaysky, and Yang (2020) , who count the frequency of terms associated with regulation in corporate disclosure files. Their data are not publicly available, and I follow the procedure from the associated working papers. Using the corpus of annual 10-K reports, the same one I used to compute cosine scores, I count the number of regulation-associated terms in each report. I then define the total count as a second proxy for regulation, 10 K i , t ⁠ . 20 Note that the first proxy is available for only 55 % of the sample (missing records in Compustat’s Business segment data), the second proxy for 37 % of the sample (missing 10-Ks in a given year), and they are jointly available for 19 % of the sample. Nevertheless, adding them as control variables does not change the results (more on the below).

To quantify regulatory burden, studies typically resort to either “supply” or “demand” data sources. The former are various textual outputs of regulatory agencies. For example, counting the number of tokens in the legal text (Mulligan and Shleifer 2005;Mulligan and Shleifer 2004;Dawson and Seater 2013), the number of restrictive phrases (Al-Ubaydli and McLaughlin 2017), and the complexity of the rule’s language (Amadxarif, Brookes, Garbarino, Patel, and Walczak 2019;Colliard and Georg 2020;De Lucio and Mora-Sanguinetti 2021). In contrast, “demand” data sources utilize information from regulated companies. For example, how much industries spend on compliance occupations (Simkovic and Zhang (2019)) and how often companies use the word “regulation” or its synonyms in their corporate disclosures (Calomiris, Mamaysky, and Yang 2020;Gong and Yannelis 2018;Wiesen and Wysocki 2015). My approach combines both supply and demand data sources: I measure the burden of each regulation using administrative sources (“supply”) and match it to regulated entities based on how they choose to describe themselves (“demand”).21This combined approach provides a solid foundation to the analysis of regulation. It accounts for the substantial heterogeneity across industries and regulations. It traces the regulatory burden back to specific regulations and federal agencies, allowing us to gain a deeper insight into the origins of regulatory intensity. Finally, the underlying data set is updated continuously, based on newly released data from OIRA, and it offers great flexibility: the burdens can be calculated at various frequencies and for subsets of regulations and agencies. Thus, the new measures can be decomposed, updated, and applied by scholars who wish to study different questions related to the economics of regulations. I discuss additional advantages of my approach relative to other existing measures in theInternet Appendix (Section A.5).

3.3 Summary statistics

Figure 4summarizes the output from the machine-learning voting classifier. It plots two histograms, reflecting the number of regulations per company (panel A) and per Fama-French 48 industry (panel B). The average company complies with1,814paperwork regulations. The distribution is right-skewed, with a small fraction of companies subject to unusually high number of regulations. Across Fama-French industries, the most heavily regulated industries include Healthcare, Defense, Aircraft, Banking, and Trading, and the least regulated ones include Textiles, Apparel, and Candy and Soda. Note that the machine-learning classifier can assign a single regulation to multiple companies and industries, consistent with the idea that some regulation transcends industry boundaries (Chen and Kalmenovitz 2020). Empirically, I find that the median regulation applies to two industries. Some of the most pervasive regulations relate to federal contracting and procurement, disclosure of lobbying activities, and importation and exportation of materials and merchandise.

Table 4lists the most burdensome regulations for selected Fama-French 48 industries.22Three takeaways can be derived from the table. First, it serves as a basic quality assurance for the machine-learning classification method. The most burdensome regulations, those that drive the variation in regulatory intensity, seem reasonably applicable to their respective industries. Some of them are likely well-known to financial economists, such as truth-in-lending regulations in the Banking industry and broker-dealer regulations in the Trading industry. Second, it illustrates the heterogeneous regulatory environment across industries. The most burdensome regulations, such as tire identification (Automobiles & Trucks), daily inspections of surface coal mines (Coal), and hospitals conditions of participation (Healthcare), are tied to specific lines of business. In other words, the table justifies the significant investment in classification. Finally, a “pure” paperwork regulation is merely an act of documentation, requiring the company to document in writing some of its activities. But the table reveals how, in reality, paperwork regulations are tightly linked to conduct rules that require the company to alter its conduct. For example, the Chemicals, Construction, and Petroleum and Gas industries are subject to various EPA regulations prohibiting or limiting environmentally harmful actions (conduct), and simultaneously requiring companies to record any such activity (paperwork). Similarly, regulators force deposit-taking institutions to refrain from suspicious transactions (conduct) and also report any such activity (paperwork). Thus, even though the official burden estimates I use (from Form 83-I) pertain only to the paperwork aspect, the measures could be viewed as a proxy for a larger subset of federal regulations.

article.4. Real Effects of Regulatory Intensity:
4.1 Hypotheses and empirical strategy

The goal of this section is to probe how regulatory intensity affects corporate-level outcomes. In the grand scheme of things, two competing theories of regulation come to mind. One theory, often labeledpublic interest, aims to explain and justify market regulation.Pigou (1938)argued that regulation is a welfare-increasing institution designed to correct market failures. Examples include monopoly power, public goods, externalities, and asymmetric information (Demsetz 1974;Melody 2016). The alternative theories, often known aspublic choice,private interest, orcapture, come in various shades but generally cast regulation as a rent-seeking process by which private actors advance their self-interests at the expense of the public good. Incumbent firms maintain their hold on power, while politicians and bureaucrats extract private benefits, such as bribes and job offers (Tullock 1967;Stigler 1971;Krueger 1974;Posner 1974;Peltzman 1976;Becker 1983;Benmelech and Moskowitz 2010).

Empirically, it is difficult to decisively accept or reject any of these perspectives. The vast heterogeneity across time periods, agencies, regional offices, and the type of regulated activities (e.g., pollution vs. collusion), invites nuanced conclusions on the efficacy of regulation. Moreover, assessing regulatory intent is not feasible. Thus, one could always argue that regulators have the public good in mind, even if the ultimate product (rules and enforcement) is not optimal. Finally, regulation is not exogenously imposed on companies. It resembles a cat-and-mouse game, where companies and regulators react to each other’s actions, and both sides continuously interact with each other through lobbying, comment letters, and meetings. Therefore, this paper does not claim to reach a final verdict on the value of regulation. Instead, it should be noted that both theories of regulation would agree that regulation is costly. Thus, a natural way to evaluate the new measure is to examine its economic impact in three main settings. I use the following specification: y i , j , t + l = α + β ⋅ R e g I n i , t + γ X → i , t + λ i + λ j , t + ϵ i , (2) where y i , j , t is outcome for firm i at time t ⁠ , and j denotes the company’s Fama-French 48 industry. The main variable of interest, R e g I n i , t ⁠ , represents firm-level regulatory intensity: number of active regulations, number of responses, and time to comply. I study three sets of outcomes. First, does regulation increase the expenses paid by companies in the course of doing business. Second, how do companies manage the regulatory burden through lobbying activities and other available tools. Third, does regulation affect the willingness of companies to invest in new projects. As explained, both theories of regulation seem to predict that greater burden (high R e g I n ⁠ ) would increase costs, encourage lobbying, and force companies to forgo potential investment opportunities.

Regulation is not randomly assigned across companies, and the interpretation ofβshould be conducted with caution. To mitigate the concerns, I use a rich set of controls and fixed effects. I add firm controls (⁠X→i,t⁠) to account for factors that are likely correlated with both regulatory burden and company policies: total assets;23market-to-book ratio, defined as the market value of equity divided by the sum of book value of equity, deferred taxes, and preferred stock; Tobin’s q, defined as the market value of equity plus the book value of assets minus book value of equity plus deferred taxes; operating cash flow; and book leverage, defined as long-term debt (dltt) plus short-term debt (dlc). Tobin’s q, cash flow, and book leverage are scaled by beginning-of-period total assets. I add firm fixed effects (⁠λi⁠) and time×industry fixed effects (⁠λt,j⁠), using the company’s Fama-French 48 industry code. This specification exploits variation within firm over time, net of industry-specific trends. Thus, the results cannot be attributed to the company’s long-run compliance costs, nor to the overall regulatory burden in a given year for a particular industry. Independent variables are lagged one period to limit concerns of reverse causality.

In addition, I consider controlling for the proxies for regulations described in Section 3.2 (CFRand 10K). This helps mitigate some of the endogeneity concerns, by focusing on the conditional impact of paperwork burden. Those two proxies are available for19%of the sample, and nevertheless all the results reported below remain economically large and statistically significant after including them in Equation2. For brevity, I report the baseline specification in the main text, and the augmented specifications are in theInternet Appendix (Table A.3, panel B).24

The baseline regressions are in annual frequency and limited to the years 1993-2020, to match the availability ofRegIni,t⁠. I winsorize all variables at the 1%and 99%levels to reduce the impact of extreme outliers. I cluster standard errors at the firm level,25and divide all continuous variables by their cross-sectional standard deviation, to facilitate the comparison of their economic magnitudes.

4.2 Costs

A surge in regulatory intensity means that the firm must satisfy more regulations that are more burdensome.26For example,regulatory capturetheories posit regulation as a rent-seeking process that imposes heavy costs on companies. Therefore, a natural first step is to test whether regulatory intensity increases costs. I estimate Equation2with SGA (sales, general, and administrative) as a dependent variable, since the salaries of compliance-related jobs would likely show up under this line item (Kalmenovitz, Lowry, and Volkova 2021). Additionally, some paperwork regulations could drive up the cost of goods sold (COGS). For example, compliance with the Federal Trade Commission’s regulations on labeling and packaging could show up as COGS rather than SGA. Moreover, as noted earlier, some of the most burdensome regulations are intertwined with conduct rules, which could directly affect COGS (seeTable 4). Both COGS and SGA are scaled by beginning-of-period total assets.

Table 6summarizes the results. Across all specifications, regulatory intensity is associated with material increases in costs, statistically significant at the1%and5%levels. The effect is identified within firm over time, net of industry trends (year×industry FE), and conditional on other determinants of costs. The effect on costs is similar across measures of intensity and it is economically significant, especially on COGS, where it nearly equals the size effect (in absolute terms). InFigure 5, I test the persistence of the effect across subsamples. First, I estimate the correlation between regulation and COGS in three-years rolling windows. I use the measure of intensity based on active regulations, and estimate Equation2with the same set of controls but without fixed effects. Panel A shows that the impact of regulation on costs is positive and significant across the entire sample period. The slight decrease over time indicates that compliance with paperwork regulation requires heavy sunk costs but less heavy maintenance costs. Finally, I estimate Equation2separately for each Fama-French 12 industry. The coefficients in panel B highlight how the positive impact of regulatory intensity on costs is large and pervasive across all industries.

Table A.3tests the robustness of the results. In panel A, I gradually add lagged values of regulatory intensity, up to four years, and find that the coefficients for past burdens are statistically insignificant. This highlights a potential transitory feature of regulatory burden, where the effect subsides over time. For instance, companies could pay high fixed costs upfront to set up compliance mechanisms. In panel B, I test the persistence of the effect by gradually adding controls. The dependent variable is COGS and regulatory intensity is based on the number of regulations. The magnitude remains relatively stable. In particular, adding two proxies for regulatory burden (⁠CFRand10K⁠) cuts the sample by nearly81%⁠. Nevertheless, the effect of regulatory intensity remains economically large and statistically significant. In panel C, I estimate an identical regression but cluster standard errors at the industry level. The standard errors decrease, indicating that the choice to cluster at the firm level yields more conservative estimates.

In sum, regulatory intensity leads to a significant rise in spending. Data limitations preclude us from pointing to specific spending on compliance, but these results are in line with the main theories of regulation, the intuition about regulatory burden, and the aggregate burdens reported in Section 2.2. Moreover, concerns about those costs led to the enactment of the Paperwork Reduction Act in the first place: reducing paperwork burden by subjecting federal agencies to a uniform monitoring process (Shapiro 2019). From another perspective, the findings in this section could be viewed as a validation exercise for the efficacy of the new regulatory intensity measures, which are indeed associated with material rise in spending.

While the results in this section are consistent with a causal interpretation, causal inference is challenging since regulatory intensity is not randomly assigned across companies. The nonrandom assignment could bias the estimated effect in different directions. On one hand, regulation could generate efficiencies and positive externalities, especially according topublic interesttheories, which reduce costs. In that case, the coefficients inTable 6would be attenuated. On the other hand, an adverse productivity shock could drive up both costs and regulatory burden. Struggling companies may face surging costs, and at the same time initiate more contacts with regulators (e.g. apply for financial support) and attract more attention from regulators (e.g. if they wish to protect the industry).27In that case, the coefficients inTable 6would be inflated. The current framework does not distinguish between the two possibilities, and I leave the causality question for future work.

4.3 Managing regulatory intensity

The results in Section 4.2 point to a significant rise in costs because of higher regulatory burden. In this section, I explore several plausible responses by companies that are subject to growing regulatory burden: political investment, in-house compliance, and capital injection. The empirical strategy utilizes the same baseline specification (Equation2), with various dependent variables that capture possible responses.Table 7reports the main results.28

Financing -the rising costs could spur changes in financing patterns. I define new dependent variables that capture three potential sources of financing:Cash⁠, which is cash holdings scaled by beginning-of-period total assets;1I(DebtIssue)⁠, which equals one if the company issued new net debt; and1I(EquityIssue)⁠, which equals one if the company issued new net equity. The latter two are calculated as inLeary and Roberts (2014): net equity issuance is the difference between sales and purchase of common and preferred stocks, and1I(EquityIssuance)=1if net equity issuance, scaled by beginning-of-period total assets, is greater than1%⁠. Net debt issuance is the difference between long-term and short-term debt at the end of the period and at the beginning of the period.1I(DebtIssuance)=1if net debt issuance, scaled by beginning-of-period total assets, is greater than1%⁠. The first four columns summarize the results. Regulatory intensity reduces cash holdings, increases the probability of issuing new debt, and lowers the probability of issuing new equity.29For example, a one-standard-deviation increase in regulatory intensity leads to1.8percentage-point increase in the probability of new debt, which is7%of the cross-sectional mean. Consequently, I document a substantial increase in the market leverage.30Put differently, companies seem to follow the pecking order to finance the rise in compliance costs.

Political investment -a growing literature documents how companies invest in political connections in order to shape regulatory decisions in their favor (Bertrand, Bombardini, and Trebbi 2014;Blanes i Vidal, Draca, and Fons-Rosen 2012;Borisov, Goldman, and Gupta 2015;Lambert 2019). In our context, a company facing intense regulatory burden could spend more on lobbying to reduce future regulatory burden, turning the “political investment” into a positive net present value (NPV) project. This is particularly consistent withregulatory capturetheories, which emphasize the power struggles and the rent-seeking aspects of the regulatory process. To test this possibility, I match the Compustat sample to lobbying data fromKim (2018). The information is collected from mandatory filings, published regulatory by the Secretary of the U.S. Senate. I define an indicator that equals one for any lobbying in a given year and use it as a dependent variable in Equation2. The results in column 5 demonstrate how a rise in regulatory intensity leads to a rise in lobbying efforts. It is consistent with the political investment channel, whereby companies invest more in lobbying activities to reduce or manage their regulatory exposure.

In-house compliance -the rise in regulatory burden could incentivize companies to shore up their in-house compliance systems: hire more compliance officers and develop technological solutions to streamline compliance efforts. It is difficult to confirm this hypothesis in the data, since I cannot separately observe compliance-related expenses. The company could also outsource its compliance tasks, or acquire start-ups with innovative compliance systems (a good example of the latter is the acquisition of Promontory Financial by IBM in 2016). With these caveats in mind, I define two new dependent variables: hiring (change in number of employees) and R&D expenses (available only for 45%of the sample), both scaled by beginning-of-period total assets. The results in columns 6 and 7 provide little evidence for the in-house compliance channel. In fact, regulatory intensity appears to decrease spending on R&D and slow hiring, although only the latter is statistically significant at conventional levels. For hiring, the effect is economically large and equals25%of the size effect.

In sum, the results inTable 7suggest that regulatory intensity spurs significant shifts in company policies. Facing higher regulatory intensity, companies accelerate their lobbying spending, slow their hiring efforts, and perhaps pull resources away from research and development. To meet their regulatory obligations, they rely on their cash reserves and issue new debt, leading to changes in their capital structure.

4.4 Capital

In this section I investigate the relation between regulatory intensity and capital investment. A long literature provides contradicting predictions. Regulation could slow capital investment because of unnecessary regulatory burden but, on the other hand, could generate positive externalities, which will improve investment opportunities. In the context of paperwork regulations, additional factors should be considered. Companies may invest in physical equipment to directly satisfy their paperwork requirements. For example, installing sensor devices to collect data on releases of toxic chemicals, or acquiring new software to track transactions in real time. On the other hand, paperwork regulations in themselves do not require changes in conduct, and thus may require only minor changes in capital expenses. Moreover, compliance costs could reduce the expected cash flows, leading the company to cancel marginally profitable projects.

To test the competing predictions, I first estimate Equation2where the dependent variable is capital expenditures scaled by beginning-of-period capital stock (gross property, plant, and equipment).31Following the standard practice in this literature, I remove from the sample financial firms (SIC codes 6000-6999), utilities (SIC codes 4900-4999), and government entities (SIC codes greater than or equal to 9000). The first three columns inTable 8summarize the results, showing a negative relation between regulation and investment (except forRegIntime⁠). The effect, however, is not statistically significant in conventional levels for any of theRegInmeasures. Put differently, the average effect is not significantly different from zero.

The lack of average effect raises the possibility of an underlying heterogeneity. Specifically, I investigate two potential mechanisms: financial constraints and uncertainty. The empirical strategy is to estimate a version of Equation 2 : y i , t + l = α + β 1 ⋅ R e g I n i , t + β 2 ⋅ ω i , t ⋅ R e g I n i , t + γ X → i , t + λ i + λ t , j + ϵ i (3) where the dependent variable is capital investment and other controls and fixed effects are as in the baseline specification. I sort companies based on a certain characteristic, ω i , t ⁠ , which predicts a differential response to regulatory intensity. The coefficient of interest, β 2 ⁠ , represents the differential impact of regulatory intensity on companies as a function of ω i , t ⁠ . With the inclusion of year × industry fixed effects, the effect is identified within-industry and net of industry-specific time trends in either regulation or investment.

4.4.1 Financial constraints

Suppose a surge in regulatory burden requires expensive compliance-related investment. A financially healthy firm could absorb the costs, while keeping its production-related investment at the same level. A financially constrained firm, though, cannot afford that. It must divert a portion of its budget toward the mandatory compliance-related investment, and subsequently cut back on production-related investment (Giroud and Mueller 2017). To test the hypothesis, I estimate Equation3and replaceωi,twith a measure of financing constraints, the KZ index (Kaplan and Zingales 1995), which I also add to the vector of firm controls. Each year, I assign firms from the same Fama-French 48 industry into two groups based on whether a firm’s measure of financing constraints lies above or below the industry’s median. If the firm’s measure of financing constraints lies above the median, thenωi,t=1⁠; otherwise,ωi,t=0⁠.

The middle three columns ofTable 8summarize the results. The loading onβ1is positive and statistically insignificant, while the loading onβ2is negative and statistically significant. These results reveal a stark difference between financially constrained and nonconstrained firms, consistent with a trade-off channel. All companies must adhere to regulatory requirements and increase compliance-related investment. However, constrained firms do so at the expense of production-related investment. Therefore, for those companies, the net effect on investment is significantly negative.32

4.4.2 Precautionary savings

A rise in regulatory burden generates uncertainty, since it is unclear how the regulations would be interpreted and enforced (Battalio and Schultz 2011).33While waiting for the uncertainty to resolve, managers have an incentive to postpone investments that can be delayed (McDonald and Siegel 1986;Bernanke 1983;Julio and Yook 2012;Gulen and Ion 2015). However, the benefit from waiting should be moderated by the ease with which the firm can reverse its investments. Suppose the firm can reverse its investments at no cost at all. Such firm has no benefit from waiting until more information about the regulations is revealed, and so its investment levels will not be substantially influenced by regulatory burden. But a firm with high irreversibility benefits more from waiting, since it has more to lose if the project proves unprofitable and downscaling is necessary, and hence should respond more strongly to fluctuations in the regulatory burden.

To test this prediction, I replaceωi,tin Equation3with a measure of irreversibility. I follow the literature and calculate the firm’s capital intensity ratio, measured as net plant, property and equipment divided by total assets. The assumption is that firms with high capital intensity tend to invest in projects that require large upfront costs, often for physical assets that are specific to their line of business. This is a rough proxy that does not explicitly consider determinants of adjustment costs, such as active secondhand markets for those assets. The common solution is to use industry-level proxies for investment irreversibility (Almeida and Campello 2007;Kim and Kung 2016). Motivated by that approach, I assign firms from the same Fama-French 48 industry into two groups based on whether a firm’s measure of irreversibility lies above or below the industry’s median. If it does, thenωi,t=1⁠; otherwise,ωi,t=0⁠.34

The results are summarized in the last three columns ofTable 8, with a significant negative loading onβ2⁠. They reveal another source of heterogeneity across companies, in terms of their response to regulatory burden: the impact of regulation on capital investment depends on which investment projects the company has. If the projects are irreversible, the company would choose to wait and see how the new regulations affects its operations. Therefore, if the regulatory burden increases, “irreversible” firms reduce their investment almost twice as much as “reversible” firms from the same Fama-French 48 industry.
article.5. Conclusion:
The notion of regulatory intensity is at the center of this paper. It is a widely discussed and perhaps one of the most fundamental concepts in economics. And yet measuring and analyzing the burden in a consistent manner proves to be a difficult task. Plenty of studies are devoted to careful analysis of specific rules and policy initiatives, but evaluating regulatory intensity as a whole remains a relatively unexplored territory. In this paper I take one step in that direction. I assemble a unique administrative data set, which systematically measures the burden of all federal paperwork regulations. I use machine-learning models to derive firm-specific measures of regulatory intensity. They represent the number of active regulations that apply to the company, and the estimated compliance costs in terms of hours and forms.

Using the new measures, I provide a novel set of facts on the evolution of regulatory intensity since the early 1980s. I further document the significant impact of regulatory intensity on firm-level expenses, financing decisions, and various business operations. I find that regulatory intensity substantially raises operating costs. To manage their rising costs, companies slow their hiring and spend more on lobbying. Moreover, they reduce cash reserves and issue new debt, leading to higher leverage. The impact of regulatory burden is especially high among companies sensitive to uncertainty and to financial pressures.

Those results reinforce the validity of the new measure, showing that it varies intuitively with firm-level outcomes. Companies subject to heavier burden behave in a way consistent with theories of regulation, from public interest to public choice. It should be noted, though, that the results do not provide a conclusive evidence in favor of either theory. First, the measures I derive are not a comprehensive summary of all federal regulations, nor do they cover pending rules that are still under development. They do, however, cover a large subset of 36,000 federal paperwork regulations, which are administered by hundreds of agencies and pertain to almost any economic activity. Second, causal inference with regards to corporate-level decisions is challenging. It is hard to imagine that regulatory intensity is randomly assigned, and I employ various strategies to mitigate those concerns. Third, this study is focused on potential costs and frictions. But a comprehensive evaluation would require additional analysis of potential benefits from regulation, which I leave for future work. While the main results described above seem to validate the usefulness of the new measures, causal inference and sweeping conclusions should be applied with great caution.

From a methodological perspective, I show how tapping into publicly available government documents can assist in improving our understanding of the regulatory process and its potential impact. The findings in this paper can serve as a benchmark for future discussions on the rise of the “regulatory state” and on its potential economic consequences. Regulation could affect business decisions in many different ways, some of which I probe in this paper. It could shape the perception of uncertainty and political risk, a topic that has been explored in recent empirical work (Battalio and Schultz 2011;Julio and Yook 2012;Gulen and Ion 2015;Baker, Bloom, and Davis 2016;Hassan, Hollander, van Lent, and Tahoun 2019). From a behavioral perspective, Richard Thaler refers to the myriad of federal paperwork requirements as a “sludge,” which prevents people from applying for benefits they are otherwise entitled to.35Hopefully, the novel measures can be utilized to better understand the propagation of regulation in the economy.
